Turn meeting notes into GitHub Issues
automatically

contextprompt joins your engineering meeting, records the conversation, scans your connected repositories, and extracts structured GitHub-ready issues with real file paths, implementation steps, and confidence levels — no copy-paste, no context loss.

Try it free

No API keys. Free plan includes 1 hour/month of recording.

Why meeting → GitHub issue is broken today

Engineering teams lose hours every week translating meeting discussion into actionable GitHub issues. The content is all there — it just never makes it into the repo.

//

Issues lack technical context

Manually created issues say "fix the auth bug" without the file that has the bug, the function that needs changing, or the approach discussed in the meeting. Developers spend 15 minutes of every issue just figuring out where to start.

?!

Meeting decisions don't survive to GitHub

The specific approach your team agreed on — which module to refactor, which test to add, which API to change — is discussed verbally and then evaporates. By the time someone creates the issue, the decision context is gone.

~>

Issue creation is a tax on senior engineers

The people who understand the codebase well enough to write good issues are the same people who should be shipping code. Post-meeting issue triage consumes the highest-leverage hours in your engineering week.

Real file paths. Real steps. Ready to ship.

contextprompt cross-references your meeting transcript against your repo map to produce issues that a developer can act on immediately.

<5m

from meeting end to issues

100%

context preserved

0

manual issue writes

Repo-aware file mapping

contextprompt scans your repositories — file paths, exported functions, TypeScript types — then maps each task discussed in the meeting to the specific file it touches. Issues arrive with the exact files a developer needs to open.

Structured implementation steps

Each extracted issue includes ordered implementation steps derived from the meeting discussion. Not "add a logout button" but "add POST /auth/logout to src/routes/auth.ts, clear the JWT cookie, redirect to /login."

Confidence levels included

contextprompt flags how confident it is in each task extraction — high, medium, or low. High-confidence issues can go directly to your backlog. Low-confidence issues are flagged for a quick human review before filing.

What a contextprompt GitHub issue looks like

This is the actual structured output contextprompt produces from a sprint planning meeting. Copy it into your GitHub issue body or hand it directly to an AI coding assistant.

contextprompt → GitHub Issue ready
Replace session tokens with JWT in auth middleware
enhancement backend auth

Discussed in sprint planning (2026-04-28). Alice raised that the current express-session implementation doesn't scale horizontally across the API pods — JWT would eliminate the shared session store dependency. Bob confirmed the existing session.ts types can be repurposed.

src/middleware/auth.ts src/types/session.ts src/routes/auth.ts
Implementation steps
  • Remove express-session dependency from src/middleware/auth.ts
  • Add jsonwebtoken sign/verify — use HS256, secret from env JWT_SECRET
  • Update JWTPayload type in src/types/session.ts (userId, email, role, iat, exp)
  • Add POST /auth/refresh endpoint in src/routes/auth.ts for token renewal
  • Update existing requireAuth middleware to verify JWT instead of session lookup
Confidence: high  ·  Source: sprint planning meeting 2026-04-28, 14:32

From meeting to GitHub issue in 4 steps

No browser extension, no native app, no audio driver. Just a meeting bot and your repos.

1
One-time setup (2 minutes)

Connect your GitHub repos

Add your repositories from the contextprompt dashboard. The tool scans file paths, function exports, and TypeScript types to build a repo map it can reference during extraction.

2
Before / during the meeting

Paste the meeting link

Drop any Zoom, Google Meet, Slack, or Teams link into contextprompt. The AI bot joins as a named participant and records the full conversation with per-speaker transcription.

3
Meeting ends

AI extracts tasks and maps to files

contextprompt cross-references the meeting transcript against your repo map. Every coding task discussed is extracted, matched to real file paths in your repos, and assigned implementation steps and a confidence level.

4
Minutes after the meeting

Copy into GitHub Issues or hand to Claude Code

Tasks appear in the contextprompt dashboard. Copy the structured output directly into a GitHub issue body, or hand it to an AI coding assistant like Claude Code or Cursor and start implementing immediately.

★★★★★
“The fact that it maps to real file paths in my repo is what sold me. I paste the output into Claude Code and it just works. We went from writing GitHub issues for 40 minutes after planning to zero.”
James L. — Full-Stack Engineer, Series B startup

Frequently asked questions

Everything you need to know about contextprompt and GitHub Issues.

Does contextprompt push issues directly to GitHub?

Not automatically — contextprompt extracts structured tasks with file paths and steps, which you can copy into your GitHub issue body or hand to an AI coding assistant. Direct GitHub push (creating issues via API) is on the Pro roadmap. In the meantime, the copy flow takes under 30 seconds per issue.

How does contextprompt know which files to reference?

You connect your repositories from the dashboard. contextprompt scans each repo's file tree, exported functions, and TypeScript types to build a repo map. When extracting tasks from the transcript, it cross-references the discussion against this map to find the most relevant files — no hallucinated paths.

Which meeting platforms are supported?

Zoom, Google Meet, Slack Huddles, and Microsoft Teams are all supported. You paste the meeting link and the contextprompt bot joins automatically — no browser extension or audio driver required.

Can I use this alongside my existing GitHub workflow?

Yes. contextprompt produces structured markdown output that drops cleanly into any GitHub issue body. There's no lock-in — the output is plain text with file paths and steps you can edit before filing.

What if the extraction misses a task or maps to the wrong file?

Every extracted issue includes a confidence level (high / medium / low). Low-confidence extractions are flagged for review. You can also view the source transcript quote that triggered each extraction and edit tasks before copying them to GitHub.
